Shahrom Sohi | Portfolio in Railway Innovation, Data and Digitalisation

Digital Transport Specialist

Portfolio of selected work across railway innovation, data ecosystems, semantic technologies, and applied AI in collaboration with ÖBB and European partners.

Focused on turning complex transport-system challenges into structured analysis, technical concepts, and operationally relevant outputs.

Railway data ecosystems and interoperability

Open data, governance, standards, and semantic modelling

Applied AI, semantics, and knowledge graphs

LLMs, structured extraction, infrastructure representation, and digital twins

Cross-border and customer mobility innovation

Booking, telematics, forecasting, and multimodal service design

1. Railway Data, Interoperability and Digital Transformation

Projects focused on how railway data is structured, published, governed, and reused across organisations and digital mobility ecosystems.

Open Data Practices in European Railways

Partner: ÖBB PV & Holding | Role: Comparative analysis of European railway open-data practices

Contribution: Mapped railway organisations in Europe that publish open data, analysed publication patterns, and identified governance and data-quality gaps across providers.

Output: Comparative overview of open-data practices and a governance-oriented assessment of how internal and external data availability can be improved.

Why it matters: Supports organisations seeking to strengthen interoperability, ecosystem visibility, and data-sharing maturity.

Railway Open Data Stakeholder Analysis

Partner context: European railway open-data ecosystem | Role: Stakeholder and use-case analysis

Contribution: Analysed the users of railway open datasets, including aggregators, app developers, researchers, and emerging mobility platforms.

Output: Stakeholder map and structured view of value creation around railway data reuse.

Why it matters: Positions open data not only as compliance or transparency work, but also as a strategic enabler of innovation and external service development.

Ticket Aggregator Analysis

Partner: ÖBB PV | Role: Comparative platform and market analysis

Contribution: Compared the platform logic used by operators and aggregators, focusing on data availability, pricing logic, and connection generation.

Output: Comparative understanding of how ticket availability and price visibility differ across operators and aggregators.

Why it matters: Supports strategic discussions around distribution, transparency, and platform competitiveness in passenger rail.

Comparative Heatmap of Railway Open-Data Availability

Visual: Heatmap showing dataset availability by company, portal, country, and macro-category across the European railway open-data landscape.

2. Applied AI, Semantics and Knowledge Graphs

Projects translating complex railway systems and documents into structured models, AI-supported extraction pipelines, and decision-ready representations.

Knowledge Graph for RINF Infrastructure Data

Partner: ÖBB INFRA | Role: Semantic modelling, implementation, and external presentation

Contribution: Designed and implemented a semantic model for RINF infrastructure data and presented it in a format accessible to external railway stakeholders.

Output: Knowledge-graph-oriented representation of infrastructure information to support consistency, interoperability, and downstream reuse.

Why it matters: Supports digital infrastructure representation and lays foundations for more interoperable railway data environments.

Public presentation: Presented at the Rail Data Forum 2025 as part of the session Infrastructure Manager as Data Innovator, representing the ÖBB–WU partnership. Event agenda

LLM Pipeline for Railway Disaster Processing

Partner: ERA | Role: Applied AI pipeline design for report processing

Contribution: Developed an LLM-based extraction approach for timelines and causal chains from ERA reports.

Output: Prototype pipeline for transforming narrative safety reports into structured analytical data.

Why it matters: Demonstrates how AI can reduce effort in safety knowledge processing and support more scalable institutional learning.

Follow-up outcome: Project selected and further funded by ERA.

Digital Twin for Customer Information Systems

Partner context: ÖBB customer information systems | Role: Data-flow modelling and systems framing

Contribution: Modelled ARAMIS/AURIS data flows for real-time operational information.

Output: Structured representation of information dependencies relevant to customer communication and operations.

Why it matters: Supports digital-twin thinking for operational information systems and highlights where service reliability depends on data quality and flow design.

LLM for Cross-Border Telematics

Partner: UIC / BCC | Role: Multilingual AI application design

Contribution: Built multilingual translation tools for railway telematics messages in cross-border settings.

Output: Prototype support for more consistent translation of operational and system-critical content.

Why it matters: Relevant for international railway coordination where message clarity, speed, and consistency affect operations.

LLM for Sales Lead Generation

Partner: ÖBB Rail Cargo Group (RCG) | Role: Applied AI prototyping

Contribution: Automated extraction and summarisation of business leads from text sources.

Output: Early-stage AI support for identifying and structuring commercially relevant information.

Why it matters: Shows the transferability of language technologies beyond infrastructure and safety into business-facing railway contexts.

3. Operations, Forecasting and Decision Support

Projects supporting more informed operational and planning decisions through data analysis, forecasting logic, and behavioural assessment.

Predictive Maintenance for Infrastructure

Partner: ÖBB INFRA | Role: Failure-pattern analysis and forecasting design

Contribution: Analysed infrastructure component failure patterns and developed forecasting-oriented approaches for maintenance planning.

Output: Data-driven basis for more proactive maintenance planning.

Why it matters: Relevant for reliability, asset management, and the shift from reactive to predictive infrastructure operations.

Passenger Demand Forecasting with Invenium Data

Partner: ÖBB PV | Role: Mobility-data analysis

Contribution: Used telecom-based mobility data to estimate origin-destination flows and load-factor patterns.

Output: Evidence base for demand estimation beyond traditional ticketing or counting sources.

Why it matters: Supports planning, capacity discussions, and service design where observed movement patterns provide additional insight.

Free-Fare Public Transport

Partner context: Public transport policy and planning | Role: Economic and behavioural assessment

Contribution: Assessed the economic and behavioural effects of fare abolition.

Output: Structured analysis of policy trade-offs and user-response implications.

Why it matters: Useful in debates where pricing, accessibility, demand shifts, and system-level effects need to be considered together.

Infrastructure Workforce Qualification Dynamics

Partner: ÖBB INFRA | Role: Workforce modelling

Contribution: Modelled workforce evolution and future competency needs in infrastructure contexts.

Output: Analytical framing for future staffing and capability planning.

Why it matters: Expands innovation work beyond technology by linking operational resilience to workforce readiness.

4. Infrastructure and Park-and-Ride Innovation

Projects on access systems, intermodality, and infrastructure use patterns, with a focus on improving planning and governance of park-and-ride assets.

Park-and-Ride Mobility Analysis

Partner: ÖBB INFRA | Role: Data analysis and governance assessment

Contribution: Analysed occupancy, turnover, and utilisation patterns in park-and-ride infrastructure.

Output: Basis for a governance-oriented tool to improve utilisation and address wider mobility effects linked to induced demand.

Why it matters: Connects infrastructure monitoring with broader questions of intermodality, access policy, and sustainable system design.

Integrating Multiple Data Sources for Park-and-Ride Operations

Partner context: Park-and-ride operations | Role: Multi-source integration and accessibility assessment

Contribution: Combined counts, sensors, and timetable data to identify bottlenecks and support operational analysis.

Output: Multi-source analytical approach complemented by walkability and bikeability assessment.

Why it matters: Helps move park-and-ride planning beyond parking capacity alone toward multimodal accessibility and station-area quality.

Onward Destinations of Park-and-Ride Users

Partner context: Park-and-ride and timetable analytics | Role: Behavioural inference and mobility analysis

Contribution: Linked parking events to timetable analytics to infer likely onward destinations.

Output: Behaviour-oriented understanding of how access infrastructure connects into wider mobility chains.

Why it matters: Supports better planning of station access, user segmentation, and intermodal service design.

5. Selected Talks, Panels and Conference Presentations

A curated selection of public speaking, panel, and workshop appearances that demonstrate external visibility across railway innovation, semantics, AI, and mobility research.

Rail Data Forum 2025, Cluj-Napoca

Role: Panelist | Context: ERA Rail Data Forum | Representation: ÖBB–WU partnership

Focus: Session 1: Digitalisation in the Railway Domain, contributing to the discussion Infrastructure Manager as Data Innovator and presenting the RINF semantic knowledge-graph work.

Why it matters: Demonstrates industry-facing representation and the ability to communicate infrastructure-data innovation in a European railway setting.

Link: Rail Data Forum 2025 agenda

Semantics Conference, Vienna

Role: Workshop holder | Context: Semantics for Transport

Focus: Organised and led a dedicated workshop on semantic technologies for transport, with emphasis on knowledge representation and interoperability in mobility and railway domains.

Why it matters: Signals topic leadership and the ability to convene technical discussion around semantics in applied transport settings.

Transport for London (TfL), London

Role: Presenter | Topic: The Potential of Open Data in European Railways

Focus: Presented on open data in the European railway sector, focusing on data availability, interoperability, and the strategic role of publication and reuse across organisations.

Why it matters: Shows relevance beyond a single national context and reinforces expertise in railway data ecosystems and external innovation visibility.

ISWC 2025 in Nara, Japan

Role: Presenter | Topic: LLMs for Investigation of Railway Disasters

Focus: Presented work on using large language models to support the investigation and analysis of railway disaster reports, with emphasis on extracting structured knowledge from complex safety documentation.

Why it matters: Connects railway safety with advanced AI methods and demonstrates visibility at a leading international semantics venue.

EWGT 2024 in Lund, Sweden

Role: Presenter | Topic: Park-and-Ride Utilisation and Parking Behaviour Analysis

Focus: Presented findings on park-and-ride utilisation patterns and parking behaviour, highlighting data-driven insights for intermodality, planning, and access-system design.

Why it matters: Reinforces applied mobility analysis experience and links infrastructure use patterns with practical planning decisions.
